topic: Vite Frontend Inventory
summary:
## Purpose
Three Vite-based frontend applications exist across two repositories. Tracking their locations, versions, and ports is necessary for coordinated upgrades (e.g., Rolldown-Vite migration) and for avoiding version-skew issues during dependency updates.

## Architecture
Three apps span two repositories:

| App | Repo | Vite Version | Dev Port | Notes |
|-----|------|--------------|----------|-------|
| `integrations/system-health-dashboard/` | coding | v5.3.1 | 3032 | Behind v6; migration risk |
| `integrations/memory-visualizer/` | coding | v6.0.0 | 8080 | On current major |
| `viewer/` (OKB integration) | rapid-automation | v6.0.0 | — | React + sigma.js graph UI |

- **Version mismatch**: `system-health-dashboard` remains on Vite v5.3.1 while the other two apps run v6.0.0. This skew blocks any cross-app migration until `system-health-dashboard` is upgraded first.
- **Rolldown-Vite evaluation**: initiated but inconclusive. Feasibility, breaking changes, and required config adjustments for all three apps have not yet been fully assessed. `wave-controller.ts` was touched during this evaluation.

## Key Files
- `integrations/system-health-dashboard/` — Vite v5.3.1 frontend; system health UI
- `integrations/memory-visualizer/` — Vite v6.0.0 frontend; memory visualization UI
- `viewer/` (rapid-automation repo) — Vite v6.0.0 frontend; OKB graph viewer (sigma.js)
- `wave-controller.ts` — modified during Rolldown-Vite evaluation

## Usage
Each app is a standalone Vite SPA. `system-health-dashboard` and `memory-visualizer` live in the `coding` monorepo under `integrations/`. The OKB `viewer/` is in the `rapid-automation` repo. Build commands follow standard Vite conventions (`npm run build` / `vite build`); `system-health-dashboard` output is served via a Docker-mounted `dist/` volume.

## Troubleshooting
- **Rolldown-Vite migration blocked**: the v5→v6 gap in `system-health-dashboard` must be resolved before a uniform Rolldown-Vite adoption is feasible. Upgrade `system-health-dashboard` to v6.0.0 first.
- **Rolldown-Vite breaking changes unknown**: evaluation was not completed. Consult voidzero.dev release notes and audit each app's Vite config (especially plugins and SSR options) before committing to migration.
- **Port conflicts in local dev**: `system-health-dashboard` binds 3032, `memory-visualizer` binds 8080. Confirm no service collisions when running multiple apps simultaneously.

metadata.topic: Vite Frontend Inventory

topic: GSM Health Warning and Statusline Rendering
summary:
## Purpose
Renders a real-time health summary in the tmux status bar for all active projects, showing emoji badges for coordinator state, knowledge pipeline, LSL health, and system warnings. Degraded state is surfaced immediately on the next refresh cycle.

## Architecture
- **Rendering chain**: tmux config → `status-line-fast.cjs` (fast path with cache borrow) → `combined-status-line.js` (full render)
- **Fast path**: `status-line-fast.cjs` borrows a sibling cache file filtered by `-w${paneWidth}.txt` suffix to avoid cross-width contamination; cache TTL is **30s**; safety timer is **10s** (exceeds CSL's internal 8s timeout so CSL can emit its own `⚠️ SYS:TIMEOUT` rather than being silently killed)
- **Cascade protection**: `getCoordinatorState` in `combined-status-line.js` is hardened against transient `curl` failures to `:3034/health/state` — a single failed probe must not degrade all coordinator-derived badges simultaneously
- **Cell-width residue buffer**: A `CELL_DRIFT_BUFFER` (+2 cells) is applied in `leftPadToStableCellWidth` to compensate for a 1-cell mismatch between `visibleCellWidth`'s counting of the ⚠️ emoji and xterm.js rendering, preventing ghost characters bleeding through
- **Emoji width fix**: The OKB/books segment uses 🟡 (not ⚠️) for warning state. ⚠️ with VS16 variation selector causes a cell-width miscalculation that makes clock text bleed past the segment boundary. All affected locations in both `combined-status-line.js` and `status-line-fast.cjs` use 🟡.
- **Heartbeat override**: Any project reporting a `'healthy'` lifecycle rollup state is always displayed with 🟢, overriding stale transcript-derived icon logic in `combined-status-line.js`.
- **Health warning** (~line 1682): `[🏥⚠️]` shown when `violationCount > 0` OR `!gcmHealthy`; source is `/tmp/.../global-status.json`
- **Idle-state badges**: `isUserActive()` helper in `combined-status-line.js` detects inactive sessions and renders `⚫` instead of `⚠️`/degraded for `📚` and `🧠` badges, semantically distinguishing idle from broken
- **Failure logging**: `csl-failures.jsonl` collects failure events; can be monitored in a loop to watch for regressions
- **Cache files**: Width-suffixed files only (e.g., `combined-status-line-cache-coding-w220.txt`)

## Key Files
- `scripts/combined-status-line.js` — full statusline renderer; health warning ~line 1682; `getCoordinatorState` cascade protection; `leftPadToStableCellWidth` residue buffer; `isUserActive()` idle detection; heartbeat 🟢 override
- `scripts/status-line-fast.cjs` — fast-path entry point; sibling borrow filtered by width suffix; 10s safety timer; 30s cache TTL
- `/tmp/.../global-status.json` — runtime health state (`violationCount`, GCM health)
- `docs-content/guides/status-line.md` — user-facing guide: residue troubleshooting, fast-path naming, failure-logging section, idle vs broken semantics (`⚫`), 3-stage latency chain (~90s worst-case)

## Usage
- tmux refreshes trigger `status-line-fast.cjs` on every status interval; the fast path serves from cache when fresh; a full CSL run is only triggered on cache miss or expiry
- Three sessions are monitored: `rapid-automations`, `coding`, `sketcher`; each has its own width-suffixed cache file
- The documented end-to-end latency is ~90s worst-case (cache TTL + coordinator probe interval + tmux refresh)

## Troubleshooting
- **Ghost/residue characters**: `CELL_DRIFT_BUFFER` may be insufficient — verify the +2 offset is present in `leftPadToStableCellWidth`; test outside VS Code (Terminal.app/iTerm2) to isolate xterm.js rendering
- **Trailing digit next to OKB segment**: Cell-width mismatch from ⚠️ emoji — confirm 🟡 is used everywhere for OKB warning state in both `combined-status-line.js` and `status-line-fast.cjs`
- **`⚠️ SYS:TIMEOUT` in status bar**: CSL is timing out only in certain pane contexts; check `csl-failures.jsonl`; verify the 10s safety timer in `status-line-fast.cjs` is correctly set
- **`[Status Offline]`**: Stale cache past the freshness threshold caused fallback to a synchronous CSL run that itself failed; check cache file timestamps and coordinator health
- **All coordinator badges degraded at once**: `getCoordinatorState` cascade — a transient curl failure to `:3034/health/state`; verify hardening is in place
- **Cross-width artifacts**: Sibling borrow is using a cache from a different pane width; verify borrow logic filters by `-w${paneWidth}.txt` suffix
- **`[🏥⚠️]` persists but GCM is healthy**: Check `violationCount` in `global-status.json`; if > 0, inspect recent observations for `[Raw]` tags
- **Project shows stale/wrong icon despite healthy rollup**: Heartbeat override should force 🟢 — verify the `'healthy'` rollup check is present in `combined-status-line.js`
- **`⚫` shown instead of expected status**: `isUserActive()` is returning false — session is idle; this is correct behavior distinguishing idle from broken

metadata.topic: GSM Health Warning and Statusline Rendering

topic: OKB Ingestion Skill (SKILL.md)
summary:
## Purpose
A versioned skill document (currently v1.3.0) that guides AI coding agents through OKB ingestion workflows, covering multiple source paths, LLM provider configuration, and error handling.

## Architecture
- **Hardlinked file**: Both paths point to the same inode — editing one edits both
- **Ingestion paths defined**:
  - Path B: Direct API ingest (pre-synthesized JSON contract)
  - Path D: Confluence ingestion
  - Path E: CodeBeamer ingestion
  - MkDocs adapter path (CLI-based)
- **LLM provider section**: Mandatory Copilot-on-VPN configuration with Docker proxy guidance (required because Node.js `fetch` ignores system proxy env vars — must use `undici` ProxyAgent)
- **Error handling table**: Six documented failure modes including Zod nullable schema failure, Copilot JSON truncation with markdown fence wrapping, and PII filter false positives

## Key Files
- `integrations/rapid-toolkit/.agents/skills/okb-ingest/SKILL.md` — canonical skill file (hardlinked)
- `.opencode/skills/okb-ingest/SKILL.md` — hardlink alias for OpenCode agent access

## Usage
- Referenced automatically by AI agents (OpenCode, Claude) when performing OKB ingestion tasks
- The Copilot VPN section must be followed when running inside a corporate network that blocks direct external API calls

## Troubleshooting
- **Copilot returns JSON wrapped in markdown fences**: Known failure mode documented in the error handling table — strip fences before JSON parsing
- **PII filter blocks valid entities**: Documented as a false-positive failure mode — review filter thresholds or bypass flags per the skill's error handling section
- **Zod schema failure on nullable fields**: Documented — ensure schema definitions include `.nullable()` where the API may return null

metadata.topic: OKB Ingestion Skill (SKILL.md)

topic: Session Log (LSL) File Management
summary:
## Purpose
Stores per-session transcript history files used to reload context across coding sessions and feed the ETM for observation generation.

## Architecture
- Files follow naming convention: `YYYY-MM-DD_HHMM-HHMM[-PART]-<hash>.md` (e.g., `2026-04-17_1100-1200-1_c197ef.md`)
- Files exceeding **200KB** are automatically split at `## Prompt Set` boundaries, inserting a part number between the time window and hash
- `.specstory/archive/` is gitignored to prevent large archive files from reaching GitHub (100MB limit)
- A Python script exists to retroactively split all 1,400+ existing oversized files into numbered parts (~8,235 total parts produced)
- `git filter-repo` was used to remove a 164MB archive file from git history

## Key Files
- `.specstory/history/` — active session log directory
- `.specstory/archive/` — archived logs (gitignored)
- `.gitignore` — must include `.specstory/archive/`

## Usage
Loaded at session start via the `/sl` skill to re-establish context. Also scanned by ETM to find active transcript files. Large files are split automatically on write; the retroactive splitter script handles historical files.

## Troubleshooting
- **GitHub push rejected (file too large)**: A file in `.specstory/` exceeds 100MB — use `git filter-repo` to remove it from history and add the path to `.gitignore`
- **ETM misses a session**: The session file may not yet exist when ETM starts — restart ETM after confirming the file exists
- **Context missing after session reload**: Check that the correct date-range LSL files were loaded; files may be split across multiple parts

topic: coding CLI — preToolUse Hook Configuration
summary:
## Purpose
Intercepts every tool invocation made by the `coding` CLI agents (Claude, CoPilot) via lifecycle hooks declared in `hooks.json`. The hook loader dispatches each lifecycle event to a JavaScript module handler; a missing handler module causes the loader to fail at initialization, denying all subsequent tool calls for the session.

## Architecture
- **Correct hook manifest**: `hooks.json` — the authoritative configuration; maps lifecycle events to handler module paths under `lib/agent-api/hooks/`
- **Legacy broken manifest**: `.github/hooks/copilot-coding.json` — references the nonexistent `scripts/copilot-hooks/` directory; was the root cause of the startup all-deny defect; not part of the working system
- **Dispatch engine**: `hook-manager.js` — reads `hooks.json`, resolves handler module paths, and invokes each handler for the appropriate lifecycle event
- **Handler pattern (`module-type`)**: each handler default-exports an `async` function accepting a context object and returning `{ allow: true, message }`; shared logging uses the `createLogger` idiom consistent with the broader codebase
- **Default handlers**: `lib/agent-api/hooks/default-handlers/startup-log.js` and `shutdown-log.js` — lifecycle logging stubs; must exist for the hook loader to initialize cleanly
- **Failure mode**: when any handler path declared in `hooks.json` cannot be resolved, `hook-manager.js` fails at initialization and denies all tool calls — including diagnostic tools — creating a self-sealing circular blocker that prevents agents from reading the files needed to fix the problem

## Key Files
- `hooks.json` — authoritative hook manifest; correct entry point for all hook configuration diagnosis
- `hook-manager.js` — hook dispatch engine; resolves and invokes module handlers; read before writing new handlers to understand the context object shape and resolution order
- `lib/agent-api/hooks/default-handlers/startup-log.js` — startup lifecycle handler stub
- `lib/agent-api/hooks/default-handlers/shutdown-log.js` — shutdown lifecycle handler stub
- `.github/hooks/copilot-coding.json` — legacy broken manifest; references nonexistent `scripts/copilot-hooks/`; not part of the working system

## Usage
The hook system fires automatically on every tool invocation during a `coding --claude` or `coding --copilot` session. Under normal operation each handler returns `{ allow: true }`. New handlers must follow the `module-type` pattern — default-export an `async` function, accept a context object, return `{ allow: true, message }`, and use `createLogger` for logging — then be declared in `hooks.json` before any session that will invoke them.

## Troubleshooting
- **All tool calls denied at session startup**: The hook loader failed to resolve a handler module; open `hooks.json` in a plain terminal and verify every declared handler path exists under `lib/agent-api/hooks/`
- **Circular blocker — agent cannot self-diagnose**: Any agent session subject to the broken hook cannot use file-read or shell tools to investigate; use a plain terminal (`cat`, `ls`, `node`) outside the `coding` CLI to inspect `hooks.json` and verify handler file existence
- **`coding --claude` silently uses CoPilot instead**: The `preToolUse` hook is denying Claude tool calls; verify that all handler paths declared in `hooks.json` resolve to existing files
- **Do not consult `.github/hooks/copilot-coding.json`**: This manifest references a nonexistent directory and is not part of the working hook system; `hooks.json` is the correct file
- **Adding a new handler**: Create the module under `lib/agent-api/hooks/`, follow the `module-type` pattern, declare the path in `hooks.json`, and confirm the file is reachable before starting any agent session
- **Inspecting dispatch logic**: Read `hook-manager.js` before writing new handlers to understand module resolution order and the exact shape of the context object passed to each handler

metadata.summary:
## Purpose
Intercepts every tool invocation made by the `coding` CLI agents (Claude, CoPilot) via lifecycle hooks declared in `hooks.json`. The hook loader dispatches each lifecycle event to a JavaScript module handler; a missing handler module causes the loader to fail at initialization, denying all subsequent tool calls for the session.

## Architecture
- **Correct hook manifest**: `hooks.json` — the authoritative configuration; maps lifecycle events to handler module paths under `lib/agent-api/hooks/`
- **Legacy broken manifest**: `.github/hooks/copilot-coding.json` — references the nonexistent `scripts/copilot-hooks/` directory; was the root cause of the startup all-deny defect; not part of the working system
- **Dispatch engine**: `hook-manager.js` — reads `hooks.json`, resolves handler module paths, and invokes each handler for the appropriate lifecycle event
- **Handler pattern (`module-type`)**: each handler default-exports an `async` function accepting a context object and returning `{ allow: true, message }`; shared logging uses the `createLogger` idiom consistent with the broader codebase
- **Default handlers**: `lib/agent-api/hooks/default-handlers/startup-log.js` and `shutdown-log.js` — lifecycle logging stubs; must exist for the hook loader to initialize cleanly
- **Failure mode**: when any handler path declared in `hooks.json` cannot be resolved, `hook-manager.js` fails at initialization and denies all tool calls — including diagnostic tools — creating a self-sealing circular blocker that prevents agents from reading the files needed to fix the problem

## Key Files
- `hooks.json` — authoritative hook manifest; correct entry point for all hook configuration diagnosis
- `hook-manager.js` — hook dispatch engine; resolves and invokes module handlers; read before writing new handlers to understand the context object shape and resolution order
- `lib/agent-api/hooks/default-handlers/startup-log.js` — startup lifecycle handler stub
- `lib/agent-api/hooks/default-handlers/shutdown-log.js` — shutdown lifecycle handler stub
- `.github/hooks/copilot-coding.json` — legacy broken manifest; references nonexistent `scripts/copilot-hooks/`; not part of the working system

## Usage
The hook system fires automatically on every tool invocation during a `coding --claude` or `coding --copilot` session. Under normal operation each handler returns `{ allow: true }`. New handlers must follow the `module-type` pattern — default-export an `async` function, accept a context object, return `{ allow: true, message }`, and use `createLogger` for logging — then be declared in `hooks.json` before any session that will invoke them.

## Troubleshooting
- **All tool calls denied at session startup**: The hook loader failed to resolve a handler module; open `hooks.json` in a plain terminal and verify every declared handler path exists under `lib/agent-api/hooks/`
- **Circular blocker — agent cannot self-diagnose**: Any agent session subject to the broken hook cannot use file-read or shell tools to investigate; use a plain terminal (`cat`, `ls`, `node`) outside the `coding` CLI to inspect `hooks.json` and verify handler file existence
- **`coding --claude` silently uses CoPilot instead**: The `preToolUse` hook is denying Claude tool calls; verify that all handler paths declared in `hooks.json` resolve to existing files
- **Do not consult `.github/hooks/copilot-coding.json`**: This manifest references a nonexistent directory and is not part of the working hook system; `hooks.json` is the correct file
- **Adding a new handler**: Create the module under `lib/agent-api/hooks/`, follow the `module-type` pattern, declare the path in `hooks.json`, and confirm the file is reachable before starting any agent session
- **Inspecting dispatch logic**: Read `hook-manager.js` before writing new handlers to understand module resolution order and the exact shape of the context object passed to each handler

topic: v7.2 Performance Measurement System — Architecture and Scoping
summary:
## Purpose
Adds measurement and policy-derivation capabilities to `coding/bin/coding` by tracking token consumption, time-to-delivery, and execution routes across all four coding agents (Claude, `gh copilot`, OpenCode, Mastra). Enables empirical comparison of agent performance and detection of off-plan execution paths (D-tours).

## Architecture
**Five interdependent subsystems:**
1. **`RunSnapshot`** — reproducibility record capturing git SHA, KB state, config, and MCP tool inventory at run start; uses agent benchmarking model: full external state pinned (model version, KB state, MCP inventory), strict reproducibility, counterfactual replay
2. **Cross-agent telemetry parity** — unified token/latency tracking; `gh copilot` CLI already routes through `rapid-llm-proxy` via a shipping `CopilotProvider` class — no interception gap there; the true gap is the **VS Code Copilot Chat extension**, where interactions bypass the proxy entirely
3. **Route reconstruction** — detects D-tours via a unified "goal sentence" as intent anchor; syntactic heuristics (loop count, edit-revert, unused reads) plus optional LLM-judge semantic scoring; LSL `events.jsonl` adapter (Phase 51) is the correct instrumentation path for Copilot-driven execution costs
4. **Success-scoring rubrics** — unified shape accommodating structured evidence (`VERIFICATION.md`, tests, code review) and diffuse evidence (goal + diff) without source bias; LLM-judge metrics deferred to v7.3
5. **km-core ontology extension** — new entity types: `Experiment`, `Run`, `Route`, `Step`, `Decision`

**Resolved design decisions:** reproducibility unit = agent benchmarking with pinned external state; D-tour detection = unified "goal sentence" with syntactic heuristics; success scoring = unified schema for structured + diffuse evidence.

**Remaining prerequisite:** Final decision on LLM-judge metrics deferral to v7.3.

## Key Files
- `project_v72_perf_measurement.md` — scoping document
- `.planning/phases/44-rest-api-git-snapshots/44-13-PLAN.md` — writer dedup cutover (Wave 5.6)
- `.planning/phases/44-rest-api-git-snapshots/44-14-PLAN.md` — server endpoints + consolidator cutover (Wave 5.6)
- `.planning/spikes/copilot-proxy-interception.md` — spike findings; VS Code Chat identified as true telemetry gap
- `rapid-llm-proxy/` — existing token tracking; `CopilotProvider` class already routes `gh copilot` CLI through proxy
- `lib/km-core/` — ontology target for new entity types (not yet added)

## Usage
No implementation code exists. Three design ambiguities have been resolved. Remaining work: finalize LLM-judge deferral decision, then run `/gsd-new-milestone` to define the phase roadmap (~6–10 phases). The LSL `events.jsonl` adapter is the designated Copilot telemetry path — do not attempt network-layer interception of the VS Code Chat extension.

## Troubleshooting
- **Copilot token data missing from proxy**: `gh copilot` CLI is already proxied via `CopilotProvider`; the gap is VS Code Copilot Chat, which requires the LSL `events.jsonl` adapter approach
- **Scope creep on reproducibility**: Decision is locked to agent benchmarking (full external state pinned); do not conflate with workflow benchmarking (internal-state-only) model
- **Route reconstruction producing false D-tour alerts**: Syntactic heuristics are primary signal; do not implement LLM-judge semantic scoring until deferral decision is finalized

topic: Health Coordinator / Tmux Statusline — ETM Spawning and Project Detection
summary:
## Purpose
The health coordinator manages ETM process lifecycle per project, feeds health state to the tmux statusline, and must correctly resolve project directory paths to find active OpenCode sessions. Incorrect path resolution silently prevents ETM from spawning for a project.

## Architecture
- **Path encoding problem**: Claude transcript directory naming collapses both `/` and `_` to `-` when encoding paths. Reverse-decoding is lossy and ambiguous. The fix is **forward-encoding**: enumerate actual disk paths first, then encode forward to match transcript directory names. Never reverse-decode.
- **ETM spawn location**: The correct place to spawn ETMs is the coordinator's tick loop in `health-coordinator.js`. `combined-status-line.js` contains dead code for this purpose — do not use it.
- **Tmux session discovery**: A bug fix was applied directly to `health-coordinator.js` for tmux session discovery bypass; this is the correct execution path (not `combined-status-line.js`)
- **`*-state.json` file pattern recognition**: Fix applied to `combined-status-line.js` for recognizing state file patterns
- **Stale duplicate coordinators**: Only the `launchctl`-managed coordinator instance should run. Worktree or manually launched instances must be killed before diagnosing health issues.
- **`supervisorctl` false error**: If `isDockerMode()` returns `true` while the verifier runs on the host, it invokes `supervisorctl` directly, producing a spurious `[ERROR] supervisorctl not found`

## Key Files
- `scripts/health-coordinator.js` — ETM spawner (tick loop), path forward-encoding, tmux session discovery, coordinator lifecycle
- `scripts/combined-status-line.js` — statusline renderer; `*-state.json` pattern recognition; dead ETM-spawn code (do not use)
- `scripts/health-verifier.js` — host-side health check runner; needs `isDockerMode()` guard
- `scripts/process-state-manager.js` — registry persistence; `readRegistry()` guarded against missing/empty registry files
- `feedback_claude_path_encoding.md` — persisted rule: lossy path encoding, references `enhanced-transcript-monitor.js:1703`
- `feedback_statusline_is_tmux.md` — persisted rule: "status line" = tmux statusline via `combined-status-line.js`
- `MEMORY.md` — pointers to both feedback files

## Usage
- After any coordinator config change, restart via `launchctl unload/load` — do not start a second instance manually
- "Status line" in this project always refers to the tmux statusline rendered by `combined-status-line.js`, not Claude Code's own UI chrome
- The `rapid-automations` project directory is at `~/Agentic/_work/rapid-automations`, not `~/Agentic/rapid-automations`
- New bug fixes belong in `health-coordinator.js` (ETM spawning, tmux session discovery) not `combined-status-line.js`

## Troubleshooting
- **Project missing from statusline**: ETM was never spawned — coordinator used reverse-decode and produced a nonexistent path. Verify forward-encoding is applied in the tick loop of `health-coordinator.js`
- **LSL marked degraded despite active session**: Same root cause — no heartbeat because ETM never started for that project
- **`[ERROR] supervisorctl not found` in health report**: `isDockerMode()` returning `true` on the host; fix detection logic or add a host-mode guard
- **Two coordinator processes running**: Kill the non-launchd instance; only the launchd-managed PID should remain
- **`[INFO] undefined` log entry**: Log call with no message body in `health-verifier.js` — add a null guard
- **obs_api crash/recovery**: SIGTERM events visible in the observations-api log; check `start-services-robust.js` EADDRINUSE retry logic and `process.ppid` in SIGTERM log lines for tracing unexpected termination sources

metadata.topic: Health Coordinator / Tmux Statusline — ETM Spawning and Project Detection

topic: Brandschutz Agent (Multi-Agent Fire Safety Training Automation)
summary:
## Purpose
Automates navigation, content extraction, and quiz completion of the "BRANDSCHUTZ FÜR BRANDSCHUTZHELFER" Articulate Rise e-learning course hosted in <COMPANY_NAME_REDACTED> SuccessFactors, replacing the earlier `brandschutz-training.sh` shell script.

## Architecture
- **Three specialized agents**: navigator, form-filler, quiz-taker — coordinated by an orchestrator agent (`orchestrator.ts`)
- **Tool layer**: `gsd-browser` CLI commands wrapped as Mastra tools
- **LLM provider**: `src/llm/provider.ts` — Copilot Enterprise OAuth (primary) with Azure/OpenAI fallbacks; uses `buildProxyFetch()` helper that reads `HTTP_PROXY` env vars and creates an `undici.ProxyAgent` to route through corporate proxy at `127.0.0.1:3128` (proxydetox must be running)
- **AI SDK version**: `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` v1.0.35 (AI SDK v5) required by Mastra v1.24; `LanguageModelV1` import must come from `@ai-sdk/provider`
- **Auth flow**: GitHub OAuth device flow → `gho_` token used directly against `https://api.githubcopilot.com` (no `copilot_internal` token exchange)
- **Key browser interaction patterns**: Shadow DOM coordinate extraction + `gsd-browser click --x --y` for UI5/JUIC elements; `gsd-browser select-frame --url-pattern` for cross-origin iframes; JS async wrapper for `evalJs` calls

## Key Files
- `brandschutz-agent/src/index.ts` — entry point with preflight validation
- `brandschutz-agent/src/orchestrator.ts` — multi-agent coordination
- `brandschutz-agent/src/llm/provider.ts` — proxy-aware provider setup
- `brandschutz-agent/src/llm/models.ts` — model definitions
- `brandschutz-training.sh` — legacy shell script (~1002 lines); still maintained as reference

## Usage
Run after ensuring proxydetox is active at `127.0.0.1:3128`. The agent performs SSO login via SAML form submission, navigates to the course player, traverses 7 interactive stations, and completes the 14-question quiz.

## Troubleshooting
- **ConnectTimeoutError to copilot API**: Proxydetox not running — start it before launching the agent
- **HTTP 400 on second agent loop iteration**: Custom proxy fetch may not be propagating through AI SDK provider chain during multi-step execution; add logging to `provider.ts` to confirm fetch wrapper is called on each request
- **gsd-browser UTF-8 panic on 'Ü'**: Non-fatal external binary issue; use JS focus+Enter or `act --intent` as fallback for affected elements
- **"Kurs fortsetzen" navigating to wrong page**: Use shadow DOM bounding-rect coordinate click (`gsd-browser click --x --y`) instead of JS `.click()` to trigger the UI5 event chain
- **SAML relay page blocking post-login**: Detect relay page and submit SAML form via JS before searching for course elements

metadata.topic: Brandschutz Agent (Multi-Agent Fire Safety Training Automation)

topic: Observations Database — Corruption Recovery and Self-Healing
summary:
## Purpose
Protects the observations store from data loss during SQLite corruption events by maintaining JSON export files as a recoverable backup and automatically restoring from them on future corruption-rotation events.

## Architecture
- **Primary store**: SQLite at `.observations/observations.db`
- **JSON exports**: `.data/observation-export/observations.json` — written by `ObservationExporter.js`; serves as the recovery source
- **Self-healing**: `SafeDatabase.js` calls `_restoreFromExportIfPossible()` automatically after any corruption-rotation event, reimporting from JSON exports without manual intervention
- **Critical schema gap**: `SafeDatabase.js` auto-restores the schema but does **not** include the `project` column added by `migrate-add-project-column.js`. After a corruption-recovery restore, the `project` column is absent, causing the digests list query to return empty while count <USER_ID_REDACTED> still return the pre-restore count (e.g., header shows 329, body shows "No digests yet")
- **Redaction corruption**: `ObservationConsolidator.js` previously applied full `_redact()` (including a broad AWS secret regex matching any 40-char alphanumeric string) to structured metadata like file paths; fix restricts full redaction to free-text fields only
- **Manual recovery script**: `scripts/restore-observations-from-export.js` — stops the service, creates a fresh SQLite DB, and reimports from JSON

## Key Files
- `.observations/observations.db` — live SQLite store
- `.data/observation-export/observations.json` — JSON backup / recovery source
- `src/live-logging/SafeDatabase.js` — self-healing restore on corruption-rotation; schema restore must include `project` column
- `scripts/migrate-add-project-column.js` — adds `project` column; must be re-run after any self-healing schema restore
- `scripts/restore-observations-from-export.js` — manual full recovery script
- `redaction-patterns.json` / `ConfigurableRedactor.js` — AWS secret regex must be scoped to avoid false positives on path segments

## Usage
`SafeDatabase.js` handles corruption automatically. Run `restore-observations-from-export.js` only when the self-healing path is insufficient. After any restore, verify the `project` column exists and re-run `migrate-add-project-column.js` if needed.

## Troubleshooting
- **Digests page shows count but empty list**: `SafeDatabase` schema restore omitted the `project` column — run `migrate-add-project-column.js` to re-add it and repopulate
- **Sudden drop in observation count**: Export JSON may have been overwritten by the exporter running after a `git checkout`; restore from git history
- **File paths corrupted in stored observations**: AWS secret regex matched path segments; run `scripts/repair-redaction-corruption.js` (dry-run first)
- **Self-healing restore not triggering**: Verify `SafeDatabase.js` calls `_restoreFromExportIfPossible()` in its corruption-rotation handler

metadata.topic: Observations Database — Corruption Recovery and Self-Healing

topic: PlantUML File Location Constraints and Documentation Style Skill
summary:
## Purpose
Enforces that all PlantUML source and rendered image files land in canonical directories. Violations caused by tools or agents writing to non-compliant paths surface as "degraded" health dashboard status with elevated violation counts. Constraint rules are scoped to prevent false positives on conversation text and tool output.

## Architecture
- **Allowed output locations** (hard rules):
  - `docs/puml/` — all `.puml` source files
  - `docs/images/` — all rendered image outputs (PNG, SVG, etc.)
- **Constraint definitions**: Three rules govern PlantUML placement: `plantuml-file-location`, `plantuml-diagram-workflow`, and `plantuml-file-organization`. Each rule carries `file_pattern` and `applies_to` scoping fields so they trigger **only on actual `.puml` and `.md` file operations**, not on conversation text or tool output that happens to mention file paths.
- **Violation trigger**: The `semantic-analysis_generate_plantuml_diagrams` MCP tool can write `.puml` and image files to arbitrary working directories during analysis runs; these land outside the canonical paths and trigger all three rules simultaneously.
- **Skill distribution**:
  - `documentation-style.md` is a Claude Code slash command (`.claude/commands/documentation-style.md`) containing a prominent **"HARD RULES"** section covering PlantUML placement.
  - `bin/coding/generate-agent-instructions.sh` propagates skills to agent-specific directories on each run.
  - Distribution to **OpenCode** (`.opencode/skills/`) and **Copilot** agents is **not confirmed** — verify `.opencode/skills/documentation-style.md` exists and contains the HARD RULES section.

## Key Files
- `.claude/commands/documentation-style.md` — canonical skill; "HARD RULES" section defines the only valid output paths
- Constraint definition files — contain `plantuml-file-location`, `plantuml-diagram-workflow`, `plantuml-file-organization` rules with `file_pattern` and `applies_to` scoping fields
- `bin/coding/generate-agent-instructions.sh` — propagates skill files to all configured agent targets; must be re-run after skill edits
- `.opencode/skills/` — OpenCode agent skill directory; confirm `documentation-style.md` is present and current
- `/tmp/.../global-status.json` — runtime health state; `violationCount > 0` triggers `[🏥⚠️]` in the statusline

## Usage
- Before invoking `semantic-analysis_generate_plantuml_diagrams`, ensure output paths point to `docs/puml/` (source) and `docs/images/` (rendered output).
- After editing `documentation-style.md`, run `generate-agent-instructions.sh` to propagate the updated rules to all agent configs.
- The scoped `file_pattern` / `applies_to` fields on constraint rules mean that ordinary conversation and tool output will not trigger false positives — rules only fire when an actual file write targets a non-compliant path.

## Troubleshooting
- **Health dashboard shows "degraded" with elevated violation count**: Search the working tree for `.puml` and image files outside `docs/puml/` and `docs/images/` — these are the likely culprits from a prior diagram generation run; move or delete them to clear violations.
- **False positives were firing on conversation text**: Ensure all three `plantuml-*` constraint rules have `file_pattern` and `applies_to` scoping fields; without these, the rules pattern-match on any text including LLM responses.
- **Violations recur after cleanup**: The agent generating diagrams does not have the `documentation-style.md` skill; confirm OpenCode and Copilot skill directories contain the latest version; manually copy if `generate-agent-instructions.sh` does not reach those targets.
- **`[🏥⚠️]` persists in statusline after moving files**: `violationCount` in `global-status.json` may not have refreshed yet; wait for the next coordinator probe cycle or restart the coordinator to force a re-check.
- **Uncertainty about which agent wrote the files**: Check the agent session log for calls to `semantic-analysis_generate_plantuml_diagrams` that did not specify compliant output paths.

topic: System Health Dashboard — Frontend Build, Deployment, and Visualization Components
summary:
## Purpose
A React frontend that renders health, token usage, and data visualizations (pie charts, treemaps, nav bar tabs) for the system-health-dashboard service. Served from a pre-built bundle inside a Docker container; source edits require an explicit rebuild to take effect.

## Architecture
- **Docker deployment**: The container at port 3032 bind-mounts `~/Agentic/coding/integrations/system-health-dashboard/dist` from the host. It serves the pre-built bundle — it does **not** watch source files or hot-reload. Any source change must be compiled to `dist/` before it is served.
- **Build target**: Builds must write to `~/Agentic/coding/integrations/system-health-dashboard/dist` specifically; writing to any other path means the running container will never serve the change.
- **Recharts `ResponsiveContainer` limitation**: `ResponsiveContainer` does not propagate `style` props to nested SVG elements. SVG overflow clipping cannot be fixed via inline styles on the container wrapper. The correct fix is to apply the Tailwind class `[&_svg]:!overflow-visible` on the chart wrapper `<div>`, which forces all nested SVG elements to render outside their clipping boundary.
- **Treemap dual tooltip**: Two tooltip sources can fire simultaneously on the Treemap component — a browser-native `<title>` SVG element and the custom `TreemapTooltip` React component. Removing the `<title>` element from the SVG eliminates the duplicate plain-text tooltip.
- **Browser caching**: After rebuilding `dist/`, the browser must be hard-refreshed (`Cmd+Shift+R`) to clear the cached bundle — the container itself does not need to be restarted because it reads directly from the bind-mounted `dist/`.

## Key Files
- `integrations/system-health-dashboard/dist/` — compiled output; bind-mounted read-only into the Docker container; must be the target of every build
- `integrations/system-health-dashboard/` — React source root; edit source here, build to `dist/`

## Usage
- Edit source files under `integrations/system-health-dashboard/src/` (or equivalent source root).
- Run the project's build command to write compiled output to `integrations/system-health-dashboard/dist/`.
- Hard-refresh the browser at port 3032 — no container restart required if the bind-mount is correctly configured.
- For pie charts with long labels (e.g., "claude-code 64%"), add `[&_svg]:!overflow-visible` to the chart wrapper and increase the container height with adequate margins.
- For treemap components, ensure no `<title>` element is present inside SVG nodes — its presence creates a second, browser-native tooltip that appears simultaneously with the custom React tooltip.

## Troubleshooting
- **Source edits not reflected in browser**: Verify the build output is targeting `integrations/system-health-dashboard/dist/` — builds written to any other path are not served by the running container. Also hard-refresh the browser to clear the cached bundle.
- **Pie chart labels clipped**: `ResponsiveContainer` cannot solve SVG overflow via `style` props. Apply `[&_svg]:!overflow-visible` Tailwind class to the wrapper `<div>` and increase `outerRadius` headroom.
- **Two tooltips appearing on Treemap**: A `<title>` element inside the SVG node is generating a browser-native plain-text tooltip alongside the custom `TreemapTooltip`; remove the `<title>` element.
- **Container at port 3032 not reflecting a fix after rebuild**: Confirm the build command was run and wrote to the correct `dist/` directory (`~/Agentic/coding/integrations/system-health-dashboard/dist`); the container bind-mounts that path directly.
- **Nav bar shifts up on Token Usage tab**: Separate regression — compare the DOM structure and CSS of the Token Usage page wrapper against other tab wrappers in `App.tsx`; look for a missing wrapper div or flex/grid layout difference (no fix applied yet).

topic: km-core Ontology Hierarchy Root Enforcement
summary:
## Purpose
Prevents writer agents from incorrectly overwriting the `ontologyClass` field on root-level entities (e.g., `CollectiveKnowledge`) with non-root types such as `"Detail"`. Misclassification silently truncates ancestry traces in the unified viewer's filter panels, causing the Online filter to stop at the project level rather than reaching the correct root. A one-shot repair script corrects any existing misclassification idempotently.

## Architecture
Three cooperating components:

- **`HIERARCHY_ROOTS` registry** (`lib/km-core/`): canonical exported list of top-level ontology class names. Acts as the reference set that all agents and guards consult when evaluating whether an entity is a hierarchy root.
- **Writer-side hard-root guard** (`integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis`): intercepts incoming `ontologyClass` write operations; rejects any attempt to overwrite an entity whose current class appears in `HIERARCHY_ROOTS`. Activated at runtime via bind-mount — when Docker rebuild is unavailable (e.g., blocked by corporate proxy), restart the MCP server via `supervisorctl` to load updated code without rebuilding the image.
- **Repair script** (`scripts/repair-ck-ontology-class.mjs`): idempotent one-shot script targeting the `CollectiveKnowledge` entity; operates in two modes — dry-run (lists affected entities, no writes) and live (flips `ontologyClass` from `"Detail"` to `"System"`). Both modes are safe to re-run.

**Verification criterion:** after live repair, the Online filter ancestry trace in the unified viewer for the CK entity must reach `CollectiveKnowledge` — not truncate at the project level.

## Key Files
- `lib/km-core/` — `HIERARCHY_ROOTS` registry exports; source of truth for root-class names
- `scripts/repair-ck-ontology-class.mjs` — idempotent repair script; dry-run + live modes; targets CK `ontologyClass` field
- `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis` — semantic-analysis agent containing the writer-side hard-root guard; activated via bind-mount + `supervisorctl` restart when Docker rebuild is blocked

## Usage
**Operator repair procedure (Plan 60-04 checkpoint):**
1. Run dry-run: inspect reported entities and confirm only the CK entity is targeted
2. Run live pass: flips `ontologyClass` from `"Detail"` to `"System"` on the CK entity
3. Visual confirmation: open the unified viewer → Online filter → verify the CK ancestry trace reaches `CollectiveKnowledge` (not truncated at project level)

The hard-root guard in the semantic-analysis agent prevents re-introduction of the misclassification going forward. All unit tests across `lib/km-core` and `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis` pass with zero regression after the guard and registry were added.

## Troubleshooting
- **Online filter ancestry truncates at the project level**: CK `ontologyClass` is likely set to `"Detail"`; run the repair script dry-run to confirm, then live pass to fix
- **Repair script re-run on already-patched entity**: the script is idempotent — safe to re-run; no duplicate changes will be written
- **Docker rebuild blocked (corporate proxy)**: do not attempt to rebuild the Docker image; restart the MCP server via `supervisorctl` to activate bind-mounted code changes (`HIERARCHY_ROOTS` export and hard-root guard)
- **Hard-root guard not preventing new misclassifications**: confirm the MCP server was restarted after the guard code was deployed; a stale process will not have the guard loaded
- **`HIERARCHY_ROOTS` export missing from km-core**: guard and repair script both depend on this export; verify the registry is exported from `lib/km-core/` before testing or running the repair script
- **Plan 60-04 worktree merge blocked**: 60-04 is an `autonomous: false` checkpoint task pinned to main-tree execution; operator must explicitly approve after verifying the dry-run output and visual ancestry trace before the orchestrator advances phase STATE

topic: Lower-Ontology Project Tagging Infrastructure
summary:
## Purpose
Provides project-level entity tagging across the knowledge management stack. A PROJECTS registry in `lib/km-core` defines valid project identifiers; a dual-stamp pattern (`metadata.project='coding'`) applied at both canonical-mapper and km-core-adapter ensures all entities carry a project tag regardless of ingest path. An L2 ontology layer of 10 coding-domain classes provides sub-L1 classification for the `'coding'` project.

## Architecture
- **PROJECTS registry**: declared in `lib/km-core`; `'coding'` is the first registered project; provides the authoritative set of valid project identifier strings
- **10 L2 coding ontology classes**: define the classification layer below L1 for the `'coding'` project domain; declared in `lib/km-core`
- **L2 classifier refinement step**: injected into the `mcp-server-semantic-analysis` pipeline immediately after L1 classification completes; assigns one of the 10 L2 classes to each entity
- **Dual-stamp pattern**: `metadata.project='coding'` written at two independent ingest points:
  - `canonical-mapper` — stamps during canonical-form transformation
  - `km-core-adapter` — stamps during km-core ingest
  - Dual coverage prevents untagged entities when one path is bypassed
- **Backfill outcome**: 2271/2271 nodes confirmed carrying `metadata.project='coding'`; achieved via backfill of 743 entities
- **docker-compose volume**: `docker/docker-compose.yml` mounts km-core at `lib/km-core`; eliminates two-clone drift that existed when a second clone was separately mounted

## Key Files
- `lib/km-core` — PROJECTS registry and 10 L2 coding ontology class definitions
- `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis` — L2 classifier refinement step injected post-L1
- `docker/docker-compose.yml` — km-core volume mount; must point to `lib/km-core`
- `.planning/phases/57-lower-ontology-project-tagging-foundation/57-HUMAN-UAT.md` — UAT tracking; two deferred items: L2 emission rate verification and post-backfill distribution check

## Usage
Project-scoped queries filter on `metadata.project`. All entity ingest paths must pass through canonical-mapper or km-core-adapter to receive the project stamp. L2 classification runs automatically post-L1 in `mcp-server-semantic-analysis`. Two UAT items (L2 emission rate, post-backfill distribution check) are open pending a full `ukb` run — operator authorization has been granted to defer these.

## Troubleshooting
- **Entities missing `metadata.project` tag**: the ingest path likely bypasses both canonical-mapper and km-core-adapter; direct km-core writes will not be stamped; route through an adapter layer or apply the stamp explicitly
- **L2 class absent after L1 classification completes**: confirm the L2 refinement step is wired after L1 in `mcp-server-semantic-analysis` pipeline configuration; check for the refinement stage in container startup logs
- **Docker km-core volume pointing to an unexpected path**: inspect `docker/docker-compose.yml` — correct mount target is `lib/km-core`; any other path reintroduces two-clone drift and may execute stale code
- **Pending UAT items not yet discharged**: L2 emission rate and distribution checks require a full `ukb` run; track against `57-HUMAN-UAT.md`

topic: LslTimelineStrip — Timeline Selection and Bucket Matching
summary:
## Purpose
`useLslSessions.ts` is the React hook that fetches and surfaces LSL (live session log) session data for timeline display in the unified viewer. It currently enforces artificial limits — a 365-day ceiling on the "all" time window and a 200-session per-query backend cap — that silently truncate historical session data, making the vast majority of on-disk session history inaccessible from the UI.

## Architecture
- **Hook**: `useLslSessions.ts` — enforces a 365-day maximum for the "all" time window; issues <USER_ID_REDACTED> to the backend LSL API
- **Backend API cap**: enforces a hard 200-session response limit per query; truncation is silent — no error, no pagination cursor, no indication of omitted records
- **Effective coverage gap**: the 30-day view surfaces only the ~200 most recent sessions; the "all" window label is a misnomer — it is capped at 365 days, not true all-time
- **On-disk ground truth**: LSL session flat files are the complete historical record; the filesystem holds the full history; the gap is entirely in the hook+API stack, not in storage
- **Pending remediation**: remove or raise the 200-session per-query limit, implement cursor-based pagination in both the hook and the backend API, and extend the "all" window ceiling to true all-time

## Key Files
- `useLslSessions.ts` — session data access hook; 365-day "all" window ceiling; no pagination support
- Backend LSL API — 200-session hard response cap per query; no cursor returned
- LSL session flat files — on-disk ground truth; complete historical record unaffected by UI limits

## Usage
Consumed by the unified viewer LSL timeline (`LslTimelineStrip.tsx`) to populate session ticks. Any time window wider than the most recent ~200 sessions silently omits older data. Consumers of session counts or date-range completeness (e.g., analytics, phase retrospectives) must account for truncation until cursor-based pagination is implemented.

## Troubleshooting
- **Timeline shows only recent sessions despite selecting a wide window**: the 200-session backend cap is the cause, not a rendering bug; the API silently returns the 200 most recent and discards the rest
- **"All" time window shows data only from the past year**: the 365-day ceiling in `useLslSessions.ts` is the cause; the label is a misnomer — fix requires extending the ceiling to true all-time in the hook
- **Session count in UI does not match on-disk file count**: expected mismatch until cursor-based pagination is implemented; the on-disk file count is the authoritative total
- **Historical sessions older than the cap are unreachable via UI**: by design of the current incomplete implementation; fix requires adding cursor/offset pagination at both the hook and API layers

metadata.scope: `useLslSessions.ts` hook and backend LSL API data access layer
metadata.topic: LslTimelineStrip — Timeline Selection and Bucket Matching
metadata.summary:
## Purpose
`useLslSessions.ts` is the React hook that fetches and surfaces LSL (live session log) session data for timeline display in the unified viewer. It currently enforces artificial limits — a 365-day ceiling on the "all" time window and a 200-session per-query backend cap — that silently truncate historical session data, making the vast majority of on-disk session history inaccessible from the UI.

## Architecture
- **Hook**: `useLslSessions.ts` — enforces a 365-day maximum for the "all" time window; issues <USER_ID_REDACTED> to the backend LSL API
- **Backend API cap**: enforces a hard 200-session response limit per query; truncation is silent — no error, no pagination cursor, no indication of omitted records
- **Effective coverage gap**: the 30-day view surfaces only the ~200 most recent sessions; the "all" window label is a misnomer — it is capped at 365 days, not true all-time
- **On-disk ground truth**: LSL session flat files are the complete historical record; the filesystem holds the full history; the gap is entirely in the hook+API stack, not in storage
- **Pending remediation**: remove or raise the 200-session per-query limit, implement cursor-based pagination in both the hook and the backend API, and extend the "all" window ceiling to true all-time

## Key Files
- `useLslSessions.ts` — session data access hook; 365-day "all" window ceiling; no pagination support
- Backend LSL API — 200-session hard response cap per query; no cursor returned
- LSL session flat files — on-disk ground truth; complete historical record unaffected by UI limits

## Usage
Consumed by the unified viewer LSL timeline (`LslTimelineStrip.tsx`) to populate session ticks. Any time window wider than the most recent ~200 sessions silently omits older data. Consumers of session counts or date-range completeness (e.g., analytics, phase retrospectives) must account for truncation until cursor-based pagination is implemented.

## Troubleshooting
- **Timeline shows only recent sessions despite selecting a wide window**: the 200-session backend cap is the cause, not a rendering bug; the API silently returns the 200 most recent and discards the rest
- **"All" time window shows data only from the past year**: the 365-day ceiling in `useLslSessions.ts` is the cause; the label is a misnomer — fix requires extending the ceiling to true all-time in the hook
- **Session count in UI does not match on-disk file count**: expected mismatch until cursor-based pagination is implemented; the on-disk file count is the authoritative total
- **Historical sessions older than the cap are unreachable via UI**: by design of the current incomplete implementation; fix requires adding cursor/offset pagination at both the hook and API layers

topic: Health Coordinator
summary:
## Purpose
LSL files are the primary verbatim record of coding sessions in the km-core project. Loading recent LSL files at the start of a new session is the standard procedure to re-establish task state, in-progress work, and likely next steps without relying on memory or external summaries.

## Architecture
- **File structure**: LSL files are stored as dated flat files. Sub-agent log files follow the convention `YYYY-MM-DD-HHHH-HHHH_S1_<index>-<ID-HASH>`; main session LSLs use analogous date-stamped naming
- **Two-tier content**: each LSL contains a verbatim transcript tier (raw turn content) and may be accompanied by or linked to observation/summary records in the observations database
- **Aggregation requirement**: individual recent sessions are often too short for sufficient context in isolation; loading the four most recent files spanning the last 1–2 days is the recommended minimum
- **Load order**: files must be loaded oldest-to-newest to reconstruct a coherent narrative and avoid interpreting decisions without their antecedents

## Key Files
- LSL files — stored in the project's session log directory (exact path resolved via `projects.json` and `defaultDir.ts`)
- `scripts/backfill-subagent-transcripts.mjs` — retrieves sub-agent LSL content for gaps not captured live
- `.planning/phases/STATE.md` — complements LSL content by recording current phase implementation state

## Usage
Used at session start to bootstrap continuity. Procedure: retrieve the 4 most recent LSL files → load oldest-to-newest → combine with `.planning/phases/STATE.md` for planning context. Read-only operation; no artifacts are changed during bootstrap. Recovery goals: (1) which tasks were active, (2) what state each task was left in, (3) what next steps are implied.

## Troubleshooting
- **Insufficient context from a single file**: Always aggregate at least 4 recent files spanning 1–2 days before concluding context is established
- **Narrative incoherence after loading**: Verify load order is oldest-to-newest; reversed order causes decisions to appear before their motivating context
- **Task details not surfaced by LSL alone**: Cross-reference `.planning/phases/STATE.md` and the relevant `*-CONTEXT.md` phase file for structured task state
- **Sub-agent turns missing from LSLs**: Run `backfill-subagent-transcripts.mjs` with a 1-day recency filter to recover sub-agent transcript content before treating the LSL record as complete

topic: VKB Graph Viewer Known Issues
summary:
## Purpose
Provides vector embeddings for knowledge graph lookups in km-core. The embedding client is an abstraction layer that allows different embedding backends to be swapped; `FastembedEmbeddingClient` is the current default.

## Architecture
- **Default client**: `FastembedEmbeddingClient` is the current default embedding client (promoted as part of Phase 42-04, sub-task D-52c)
- **Consumer**: `KnowledgeQueryService.js` is the primary consumer; it uses embeddings to execute similarity searches against the knowledge graph
- **Phase context**: D-52 is an active sub-task sequence within Phase 42-04; working tree is clean after D-52c; the next D-52 sub-task is identified by consulting `.planning/`

## Key Files
- `KnowledgeQueryService.js` — knowledge query service; instantiates/consumes the embedding client for vector similarity lookups
- `.planning/phases/` — phase planning directory; consult for the next D-52 sub-task and Phase 42-04 scope

## Usage
Embedding is triggered whenever `KnowledgeQueryService` performs a similarity-based knowledge lookup. The default client (`FastembedEmbeddingClient`) runs locally without an external API call. Switching the default client requires updating the client factory or DI wiring consumed by `KnowledgeQueryService.js`.

## Troubleshooting
- **Embedding client not found after default switch**: confirm `FastembedEmbeddingClient` is correctly wired as the default in the client factory/instantiation path consumed by `KnowledgeQueryService.js`
- **Next D-52 sub-task unknown**: working tree is clean after D-52c; read `.planning/` (specifically the Phase 42-04 context and STATE file) to identify the next pending sub-task before writing new code
- **Slow or failing local embeddings**: `FastembedEmbeddingClient` runs a local model; verify the model file is present and the fastembed native dependency is correctly installed for the current platform

metadata.topic: VKB Graph Viewer Known Issues

topic: Concurrent Agent Session Management — Phantom Commits and Inter-Session Communication
summary:
## Purpose
Documents two coupled hazards that arise when multiple Claude Code agent sessions run concurrently on the same repository: phantom commits (unauthorized changes appended by a runaway parallel session) and inter-session communication gaps (operator approvals delivered to a prior task instance, causing the active agent to revert intentionally-approved changes).

## Architecture
**Phantom commit pattern:**
- A runaway parallel session appends commits under the same task ID and git author as the active session, often minutes apart
- These commits may re-revert previously authorized changes, creating a repeating loop: commit → revert → report old symptoms
- Loop confirmation: the same commit hashes appear reverted and re-applied across multiple rounds with no net progress

**Inter-session communication gap:**
- Operator feedback delivered to a prior task instance (identified by its task ID, e.g. `ae273b46be3037ea6`) is not visible to the current session
- The active agent, lacking visibility into the operator's approval, treats approved commits as unauthorized and reverts them — directly conflicting with operator intent
- Detection signal: `git log` shows a repeating revert/re-apply pattern; the active session has no corresponding approval record

**Phantom session detection checklist:**
- `tmux ls` — lists active tmux sessions; look for unexpected Claude Code sessions
- `lsof .git/HEAD` — identifies processes currently holding the git HEAD file open
- `ps aux | grep claude` / Activity Monitor — finds running Claude Code processes
- IDE background process inspection — IDE-spawned agents may not appear in tmux
- `launchctl list | grep -i claude` — finds daemon/agent processes registered with launchd

**Remediation sequence:**
- Identify phantom commits via `git log` (same task-id, same author, minutes-apart timing during periods the active session was idle)
- Revert in **reverse chronological order** (newest first) using `git revert` to preserve the audit trail; produces one revert commit per phantom commit
- Verify build/TypeScript compilation is clean post-revert
- Confirm any previously-authorized changes are correctly restored after the full revert sequence

## Key Files
- `.git/HEAD` — inspect with `lsof` to find concurrent git-accessing processes
- No specific source files — this is an operational workflow pattern, not a code subsystem

## Usage
Apply this workflow when:
1. Unexpected commits appear in `git log` not explicitly authored by the active session
2. A commit → revert → symptom cycle repeats across multiple rounds without resolution
3. Operator feedback appears to have been acknowledged elsewhere but the active session keeps undoing approved changes

Before reverting any commit, confirm it is genuinely unauthorized by cross-checking against operator-approved task lists. When the revert trigger is ambiguous, **block on further code changes** and request concrete evidence (screenshots, process logs, CI output) rather than writing into another potentially futile cycle.

## Troubleshooting
- **Repeating commit → revert cycle with no resolution**: Treat as a strong indicator of a live conflicting session or a communication gap — stop writing code; diagnose the session landscape first
- **Authorized changes being reverted by active session**: Verify whether operator approval was delivered to a different task instance ID; the active session has no cross-instance visibility by default
- **Phantom commits from same author/task-id**: Run `tmux ls`, `lsof .git/HEAD`, and `ps aux | grep claude` before reverting; terminate the rogue process to prevent re-introduction after revert
- **Post-revert compilation errors**: Confirm reverts were applied newest-first; out-of-order reverts produce merge conflicts that break the build
- **Cannot locate the phantom session**: Check `launchctl list | grep -i claude` and IDE background agent panels; IDE-spawned sessions are invisible to tmux
- **LLS suppression or other fixes cannot stabilize in the repo**: If a specific fix has been reverted multiple times across sessions, treat stabilization as blocked until the parallel session hazard is fully resolved

metadata.scope: Detection and remediation of unauthorized commits from runaway parallel Claude Code sessions, and the communication gap hazard when operator feedback reaches a stale session instead of the active one.
